video_title: Quantum 101 (with Chris Miller and Zach Yerushalmi)
transcript: Quantum, a 101, why it matters, what victory looks like. To discuss, we have on Zach Eru Shalmi, chief executive of Elevate Quantum, a public-private consortium based in the Mountain West that I will let him explain. Co-hosting, as always it seems almost, Chris Miller. Zach, welcome to China Talk. All right, Quantum 101, why should China Talk listeners uh turn their uh light-years to Quantum? >> China Talk listeners should should care about Quantum because it you know, with AI on our doorstep, Quantum is the single biggest lever that we have to pull, honestly, as a society for about the next couple of decades. Um and particularly so candidly for the China Talk listeners, it's not just a a big economic national security opportunity, but for such a policy-oriented group of folks, I I think it's one where um because the margin of error is so thin, we uh have more at stake here than any industrial program since the atomic bomb. >> What what do you mean by margin of error? >> So, you know, there two folks that on this call on this on this podcast has spent a lot of time thinking about semiconductors. My observation there is in semicon, we have literally decades of moat over China and other folks that we care about competing with. And that doesn't mean we sleep on semicon. That doesn't mean we forget industrial policy there. But Quantum, Quantum's kind of fundamentally new for a lot of the capabilities we're trying to bring forward, and that by definition means that our moat is pretty darn small. And so, whereas in semicon we we get things right and a little bit wrong and da da in quantum I think our margin of error is freakishly small. And so we have to get it right from the beginning. >> All right, make the case for why it matters. Well, why is this the thing that could is the next door for humanity to open over the next half century? >> So, why it's so important goes back to actually the the inception of the idea of a quantum computer. It came in 1981, this guy Richard Feynman. He has this famous quote of uh of effectively like nature is quantum, damn it. I hope I can swear. And it's the realization that if we want to solve the problems that quantum mechanics governs, which are really the world of the atomic realm, not Marvel, but the very small, the very cold. This is like drug discovery. This is catalyst. This is material science. This is all the things that govern the building blocks of the universe. We can't use our classical approach in classical computers to go and solve that. Um and and this gets a little bit into the math and could get a little bit dangerous, but um the the quantum world behaves in ways where even very small systems explode in complexity. So, uh a standard two-particle system these systems explode at two to the end. So, if you have a two-atom system, that's two to the end states that you need to understand. If you add a third atom to that, it's two to the third. So, you suddenly need to understand eight states, not one additional. By the time you get to 20 atom systems, you need to understand a million states in order to do that. And a 20-atom system is freakishly small. So, it gets back to why does this matter? Let's use this the specific example, penicillin. Penicillin is 42 atoms. That is not a big molecular system to it, but penicillin is obviously pretty important. If we want to understand penicillin, much less where it falls short, then in order to do that classically, using our kind of current computing paradigm, even with the world's best AI, we'd have to use something like 10 to the 86 transistors to do it. And just to double click on that, like I said 10 to the 86 quickly, it's a shockingly large number. 10 to the 86 would be needing more transistors than there are atoms in the observable universe. Like in kind of simple terms, we could literally use like the energy of the entire universe, and we couldn't quite make a basic model physics-based for penicillin. And so if we ever talk about living in the Jetsons age, right? Rationally designing all these things, our current paradigm, even with the world's best AI, is just never going to get it there. And so it gets back to that quote from Feynman. In '81, he just went on this rant. And it was really a thought experiment, which is nature is quantum mechanical, damn it. What if we're just approaching this on the wrong terms? What if we built a computer that operated on the same principles as penicillin operates itself? It'd probably be more efficient. And it wasn't just a little more efficient. It was a reinvention of what a computer could be. Instead of needing more atoms, sorry, more transistors than there are atoms in the observable universe, you need something like 186 of these quantum bits, qubits. Which we're not there yet, but we're more or less on the cusp of. And if you can do that again, because these systems are exponential in nature, when you go from 186 qubits to understand penicillin to 187, just one additional system, it's not a little bit better computer. It is thinking about penicillin interacting with its neighbor. And when you get to 1,000 qubit systems, you're talking about rational design of much more complex systems. And so if we ever want to get to what I think actually even candidly folks talk about the AI world of curing cancer, solving climate change, addressing the material science of the world all around us, actually really the the the only cowbell that we can really hit on for some of those problems is quantum. >> Could I ask maybe the same question from an economics perspective, which is thinking about the the kind of market for quantum computing capabilities, and is it an unfair question to ask cuz if you'd have asked and people at Open AI in 2019, what's the market for AI, they would have given you a very large number without much justification cuz who knew exactly how it would have would have played out, but how do you think about where we'd like to be deploying quantum computing capabilities in 2035? Drug [snorts] discovery is obviously the first answer everyone gives, but and that's obviously a potentially huge market, but but beyond that, how do we think about the economic impact of quantum computing? >> Yeah. So, there are two applications that that folks go to. So, the couple of applications, the first would actually maybe not even be life sciences. It would be something around corrosion. An accurate corrosion modeling, which is worth tens of billions of dollars to the global shipping and national security sector. And that's because it's a more simple problem and tractable to get around. There's some interesting things around nuclear chemistry, and as these systems get bigger, then you start to look at drug discovery. You start to look at material science. Folks a lot talk about room temperature superconductors, which is a whole other, you know, YouTube wormhole to get into, but if we want um cell phone batteries that never lose power, right? It's the ability to rationally design these systems at the molecular level, at the atomic level, opens up that. The second class of problems from an economic impact perspective, um candidly is a is in many ways maybe more near-term and more scary, which is gets back to the transistor analogy. Most of the classical algorithms we developed occurred after we had the computer. The only other big class of problems that folks know that quantum computers are useful for, aside from the molecular modeling piece for the physics modeling piece to it, is um they can they're really good at the hidden subgroup problem, which sounds a bit jargony cuz it's a bit jargony. The big one there is factoring large primes. And for anybody aware for cybersecurity concerns, if you can factor large primes, you basically cracked the code that underpins all of our existing cybersecurity infrastructure, which is why global governments, even putting aside the Jetsons nature of what we can unlock, are really worried because all of their codes and all of our financial infrastructure and things like Bitcoin are underpinned by this problem that a classical computer literally takes age of the universe to solve and a quantum computer looks at, laughs at, and um you know, steals your Bitcoin wallet. >> All right. So, before we go too deep into applications, Zach, what's your favorite analogy to give folks to start wrapping their head around this? >> So, the best analogy that I've seen is this maze analogy that folks should look up online by Matt Lanjone he's a he's the quantum partner at BCG. And what he talks about is take a maze, right? Kind of hold it in your head. Um the way humans approach a maze is actually pretty similar to how classical computers do it. You walk in, you're faced with the decision to go left or right, you go right, you bump up against the wall, afterwards you kind of walk back, and the time it takes for you to get to mazes the time it takes to make each of those decisions and then work your way through the maze. A quantum computer approaches a maze fundamentally differently. Quantum computer walks in the maze, looks at that decision to go left or right, because it can operate in principles of superposition and entanglement and interference, it says, "Yes." It explores both paths simultaneously. And not just at that single node, it looks across every single node of that maze and in parallel makes that same exploration of paths. And whereas again that classical computer takes you the time to make each decision and then the accumulation in sequence of each of those decisions, a quantum computer looks both at that first and then each every other node and goes, "Yes, let's go for it." And so all of these questions of out there in the real world for applications, economic impact, those questions that look like the maze are those of the molecular world and worth, you know, again, the hundreds of billions to trillions of dollars cuz it turns out that whether it be chemistry or molecular science design, they they actually have a lot of similar characteristics characteristics um to what we just described. >> So where were we, I guess if if 2025 is our turning point, where were we before? What what where were the past Where was 2014 to 20 24? So uh quantum computer started as a thought experiment in 1981. The the entire industry was kind of born in that moment when when Feynman was like, "You know, let's build something on nature's own terms." In the uh it was 2009 that what 2009 the first two two cubic gate operation um which was an important great breakthrough that actually happened in in Colorado as a kind of unitized thing for how you would create a quantum processing unit. And we've had not just steady, but I'd argue exponential progress in the capability of these systems um since that time. Um you had what was it 2019 2020 uh that the the quantum supremacy paper um came through with with um Google and then it was last year that you had the sea change where I I'd argue it's a shift from the more fundamental side of R&D to something where you're really just focused on the engineering of these systems to a sufficient scale that these problems that keep the NSA up at night and then the rest of us dreaming from a from a new capability perspective really on the cusp. And then if we look at industry timelines, you know, even a couple years ago folks would have said that quite a useful quantum computer is about 10 years out. Last year that went from, you know, 10 plus years out to with these breakthroughs it's on a you know, three to five year basis that folks think we'll get these these systems that are capable of of you know, whether it be the cryptographical capabilities that were were worried about candidly or on the other side some of the the material science capabilities that open up new economic opportunities. So it's it's happening fast. >> So I think I agree that we we've shifted from the the realm of science experiment to the realm of engineering. Um and the question that brings up is how should policy change? >> Yeah, so uh two things that I would I would say um and I've I've said this before but but your take on it and learning from other industries I think is essential. That's what I'm always trying to figure out. Um >> [snorts] >> the first is if we use the computing um history as as a lesson um the fundamental you can't just like put down the fundamental R&D to that. Um if we from a computing age got to vacuum tubes and put our tools down and said job's done, we would have missed out on the transistor. So that engine of growth and innovation of thinking what is the next generation and maybe even a reinvention of what people are thinking from a basic architecture is essential. And then the second piece of that, so fine, how do we look at um uh the industry now that there's this sea change, the mental model that I always use for quantum is biotech. Um and in biotech, um you have drugs. Um uh maybe it'd be small molecule, or you have CAR-T, or these different sorts of modalities. You have that for quantum itself, and those are the things that, you know, will cure cancer and yada yada. You also have the tools um for um addressing that. And as we cross into this chasm right now, what I would think about from a policy lens is we've kind of entered phase one clinical trials. And so what the things we need to do are, from a policy maker perspective, is actually it looks like a similar tool set to how we foster the right environment for biotech itself. The important distinction, though, is in biotech, again, back to that, we have a wild moat, right? The cluster, both commercial and scientifically, around Boston is so globally dominant that we can screw up a bunch of stuff. And so now it's a what are the tools we need to do um given the same sort of architecture, right? Material technical risk, you need to get commercial payout, you need to do these things, but now we need to do that with a much earlier stage industry. >> Well, let's talk about let's do a little bit of industry mapping because you do have these very shiny releases by um some of America's largest publicly listed companies, and you've got some startups doing stuff, and you got the government labs, and you got academia. Let's start with the giant companies. I mean, is this just like everyone wants to be Bell Labs? Like, why is why is Microsoft and Google spending time on this sort of thing? They got data centers to build with their capex, right? >> Them spending a couple billion dollars on it a year to make sure they have their finger on the pulse so that there's not a Sputnik moment for them as a company is just worth the ROI. And and ultimately, they have big quantum programs, arguably some of the largest, but relative to everything else they're doing, it's rounding errors on their balance sheet. Um and and I'd argue the same about the US government, right? DARPA, indicative of how important this is, um DARPA's largest public program in the agency's history is quantum. It's called the Quantum Benchmarking Initiative. And it almost gets a little bit to your question, Chris, on now that we're in this new era, what are the policy levers that we need to undertake to get this right? That's canonical. And it all comes back to what are the right commercial cuz they're commercial structuring, that's what makes biotech motor on, and then what are the right technical levers that you need. >> Uh do you want to explain what the uh what DARPA's Quantum Benchmarking Initiative actually is because it it actually seems to me exactly like the clinical trial analogy that you just mentioned? So, can you dig into that? >> Yeah, so um So, the Quantum Benchmarking Initiative uh as I alluded to it, large public program in the agency's history. I think this year alone they're looking to spend $600 million on that. Um on that. The frame there is uh you know, they don't say this explicitly, but um the US government can't afford to be surprised that China can break its codes. And so, they stood up this program that is effectively about how to learn about the capabilities of the leading players in the world sans China cuz China's not going to um uh talk to the US government about its quantum capabilities. And then the model that it took to do that, um and huge respect for the team there, is actually a kind of reconstructed an advanced market commitment for um what you see in drug discovery, but applied that to quantum. And so, it's effectively like a grand challenge prize structure. The first phase, you get a little bit of money, and it's like a you know a million dollars here or there and it's really just a table stakes thing. The next phase is 15 to 20 million dollars and that's if you pass a certain scientific threshold. But then things really get going by the third phase of the program where you can get paid 300 million dollars if your system is deemed credible to that stage and you build a demonstration scale capability for it and then the implied thing is after that, right? You get through that third phase of DARPA, it's kind of your FDA approval. Some government agency is going to buy one of these things cuz it's it's going to investigate parts of the cryptographical world that they would be very interested in. And actually if we get back to it and it gets back to that that frame, those payouts map really well to the different sorts of commercial staging you'd see in biotech. Um 300 million dollar prize that you know some phase one two stage and then a billion dollar prize if you get past that. And that's super important not just cuz these companies want to earn money but because if you want a virtuous cycle going, you need to get the venture investors in the private markets excited and those payouts are big enough in order to create the incentive around it. >> All right, so let's go to startups. >> Yeah. >> Why do they exist? Are they real yet? What are they doing? >> Yeah, yeah. Um so Um startups exist here just like startups exist in biotech, right? Big old companies, IBM is putting putting in billions of dollars into this thing and IBM is um It is the flagship program in quantum. But big companies face um They can do a lot of innovating and so the innovating and in this market is like you see in biotech where you get the big tech players, they are driving programs, they have amazing access to super compute, but there are newer stage programs, newer stage approaches that could be incredibly disruptive and those are typically pioneered by startups. And if those really take off, then typically the big tech players swoop in and either do an investment or buy that out. And we've seen that in quantum. There are a couple of approaches in quantum computing, call modalities. The historic one is called superconducting uh what's the solid state of her but superconducting qubits. Um this is the thing that John Martinez won the Nobel Prize for recently. That's gotten 60 to 80% of the investment to date for the industry. But there are newer approaches. The most prominent of them is probably neutral atoms. That instead of using almost a synthetic qubit quantum bit, they use atoms themselves as the qubit. And this was kind of science fiction literally 3 4 5 years ago of people who thought that this idea was a total joke. And it was a bunch of startups who, you know, like you see everywhere else in the world, kind of grabbed the mantle and said, "No, like you know, I did my post doc on this. This is This is not a total joke." Um and ran at that. And it And there was a sea change probably 2 or 3 years ago. And it's now one of the leading approaches. And nobody would have bet on that um that little time back. And now it's one of the things that could really have a shot. A marker out this is Google as one example. Um they pioneered and and continue to push forward superconducting um as their core approach, but they're so worried and interested in neutral atoms, they just gave QuEra $250 could work as well. So that's the role that startups can play. It's the classic disruptive innovation and and driving forward um what folks thought couldn't be possible because the risk reward wouldn't make sense for an existing company. >> I think now is time for a little Elevate E Tour. Won't you [snorts] tell Tell the folks out there what you do all day, Zach. >> Um So, uh Elevate uh is the US government's quantum tech hub. So, we're the first and only major place-based investment that the US government has made in the quantum industry. Um Now, they did that ultimately because what one reason had nothing to do with us. The The Mountain West cluster is the largest quantum cluster on the planet. Um it it's almost it's probably close to half of US quantum jobs, half of deployed capital. It's It's massive by by quantum standards, right? It's small industry, but still massive by quantum standards. But then the second well, I do and it gets back actually to the policy measures that I be keen on. Um our mission is dramatically accelerate the commercialization of quantum, and we do that as a public-private partnership. And the doing of that is focused on specific typically technical bottlenecks that are market failures that other players aren't well placed to solve. So, we look at things like fabs. We look at things like packaging. We look at things like certain shared use equipment that for a whole bunch of reasons, national labs and universities aren't well placed to address. And then startups might not have the capital, or they might not have the expertise, or they might not have the time in order to go and solve for those. And that's ultimately what Elevate, which we have a 140 150 members in our consortium, everybody usual suspects between the national labs and the universities in the Mountain West, but then also every big tech player with a quantum program. That's That's what we dive in to solve with with lots of friends. >> So So, Zach, so we're we're we're in this quantum race. Uh China's a major competitor. What does winning actually look like? >> Um in my head, it's getting there first and maintaining the best capability as a nation long into the future. Getting there first is building the first Folks will throw out the word fault tolerant, but think of that as a commercially useful system. Something that uh whether it be the cryptographical use cases or the material science use cases or any of those things does something that drives commercial value. This is like building the first useful computer with a vacuum tube or something like that. But then the the second is continuing to have the best capability or really ultimately I guess maybe access to that. That's what I think winning means. >> Uh let me start. One of the uses of quantum computing that we've known about for a long time, commonly discussed, is breaking encryption. Um >> [snorts] >> now we've got uh post-quantum uh encryption standards that have been released by NIST, although I think it's unclear how widely or rapidly they're actually being deployed, probably not rapidly enough. Uh walk us through how you see uh us reaching a point in which uh all of our uh 2010 era encryption uh is easily broken by a quantum computer. >> Yeah, [snorts] it it's actually it's it's pretty scary cuz uh NIST I think it's all government systems by 2028, 2029, but then the rest of consumer systems by 2035, but um you know, that that recommendation I think came out early last year. By the end of last year, folks are talking about three to five years from last year, so 2030, that will have these systems online that can break these standards. That freaks me out um because typically you want 10 to 15 years as an upgrade cycle um for for traditional security protocols, and we have three to five. Um so, that's a bit worrying. Why these systems can um do that is through this algorithm discovered by Peter Shor. It nothing to do with the original um idea behind a quantum computer. Um I spoke about it before. It They're They're good at the hidden subgroup problem. And the two prominent techniques of the hidden subgroup problem that classical computers suck at, which is why they're used as a basis for all these encryption standards. One is factoring large primes. Um so if you have 15 out there exposed as a public key, um through a you know, a weird fact of math, um it takes a normal computer a really long time to figure out that you could break that into three and five. And the bigger that number gets, the longer that computer takes to get it. And the other one is is something called elliptical curve cryptography, which is actually kind of a similar problem, but it it's really cool math and it and it and it um it it sounds like what it is. Um it's basically using um elliptical curves as as a as a way to find a hidden subgroup. And that is the basis for a lot of other types of cryptography like the um uh to help secure the signature for Bitcoin. And so uh normal computer looks at these things, has a really hard time, again, age of the universe, in order to break that down and understand it. Um whereas a quantum computer, because it has this exponential speed up on a 3 to 5-year timeline, would be able to solve that hidden subgroup problem. And so we face the cryptographical standards that we have. And the the thing that worries me as well, it's not just that we have a wildly short time to move to a new cryptographical standard. It is that lattice-based encryption, which is the standard that folks are saying we should move to, which is put out by NIST, and the theory behind that is is very good, and it and folks think that a quantum computer would have a really hard time addressing those encryption standards. The issue there is theory is very mature. Implementation is really not mature. And so we both have to move faster than we really ever have to a new encryption standard, but the one that we're moving to um it's wrong to say the kinks haven't been worked out, but it just really hasn't been deployed at any real scale. And you put those two things together, and um it's something I worry about. It's something a lot of people I think worry about. >> I think on the encryption part, I think the the thing that I haven't fully thought through is like for AI, like there's this AI race, but like the fruits of it are kind of far out, like it's productivity enhancements, and whereas for decryption, the fruits are like immediate if you get there. And so, you know, how do governments both in US and China think about like if we're 6 months away um like we're never going to be 6 months away from the fruits of AI cuz it'll always be kind of constantly bearing fruit, but if you're 6 months away from decryption, like, you know, at what point do you just say all quantum computing resources must be devoted to this task? Um defense production act style. You know, it seems highly plausible China would do that, and it seems possible we'd do that, too, if the if that were the stakes. So, there's an interesting game theory around around that dynamic. >> Like bomb the data centers was like the sort of not serious or maybe some people thought it was serious. I thought it was a not serious kind of meme from 2022 or 2023 about like what if AI gets out of control, but like you know, it starts to become a little bit more plausible in the in the quantum space if the stakes are that all cybersecurity disappears. >> So so >> Well, it kind of seems harder to bomb a quantum computer, though, right? Cuz it's just like one >> room. >> You can put it anywhere. >> And the know-how presumably continues even if you destroy the physical uh device. >> I I am trying to figure out you know, there's like stuff around nuclear chemistry, which is really scary for for quantum computers. Um that's one of the many reasons that um folks care about them. Um and again, none of this is on the high side. Um the what's the game theory of Do you think it's that different from we're 6 months out from AGI? >> Well, I think if if you think that AGI is like a a a threshold that before you have nothing and afterwards you have superintelligence, then I think the game theory is similar. But I don't think we really believe that there's a sort of AGI threshold that's has a dramatic before and after relative to a ongoing gradient where you get better and better capabilities with more and more productivity. And I think for most kind of economic applications of quantum, it looks like again, that's I don't know, steady is the right word, but um a a trend over time. But the decryption is is this threshold dynamic where if you're on the wrong side of it, you're the stakes are high. >> So so the one thing I'd say there, so yes, absolutely. Stakes are super high. Uh uh global Western governments um uh they committed something like 23 billion dollars to quantum in the last 3 years. Um and I'm I'm sure they're really excited about molecular modeling, but they're probably mostly really scared about um the encryption side of it. >> [snorts] >> The one thing that I would call out is the first computer that gets there is not going to be very efficient at breaking those codes. It could literally, depending on the architecture, it could take like a month or many months in order to break one code. Um which means you got to choose your bullets very very uh assiduously. Um and that's depending on the architecture, but I do think it is a little bit more akin to your AI model of just because you got there doesn't mean that there's more juice to squeeze. Um I think the thing that I worry about >> But but isn't the first code that you break but isn't the first code you break like the Chinese nuclear codes? It's a pretty high value code. Like I I feel like there's some pretty high value codes you could you could break right away and justify thinking about it as a pretty important I mean I don't know anything about the Chinese nuclear system. But like that seems like exactly where one would go um if one was going to think about the high value code. >> Yeah, there there are people with um clearances far above my my pay grade that probably know how much concentration there is um for that that Chinese high value code. Um I don't have a good sense there. I think the thing that I worry most about from a quantum computer aside from the code breaking is the recursive nature of these systems and how they how they improve existing material science stuff. So, take the example of high temperature superconductors or things in nuclear chemistry, right? If you had a system that could rationally design uh superconductors, a system that could rationally design things in the chemistry world, you would use that and lock down IP space and know-how in a way that would block out um uh uh adversaries and competitors. And so that's I think where from a moat perspective it's not just how you build the system, but how you lock down on the things that that system invents. And because you know AI is a lot like stabbing the dark, right? It's still really really really good fancy curve fitting. Quantum computers are are not that. They're not they're not guessing. It's ab initio. They're solving these problems from a first principle basis and locking in on the right solution for that thing. Um and if I'm thinking of like you know, silent mushroom cloud ask sort of analogies to it. That's That's where my brain gets going a little bit scary. I think. >> Well, let's talk about like Okay, that So, that first computer um I mean, the like it it won't be an engineering challenge like a Manhattan project that like ends up costing a hundred times more than any other past one presumably, right? I mean, is it more likely to be some sort of sort of engineering breakthrough or is money a thing that you can like brute force your way into having that um quantum computer that's actually useful to help you break the nuclear code? >> Yeah, so um you know, honestly, especially cuz we crossed that threshold where every qubit that you add um is is now making the system more powerful, you can just throw money at the problem. You'd build a wildly inefficient, wildly expensive computer, but it could like break RSA that that that couple of times. Um so, that would be super bad news. How you spend the money is super important. I think that and this is maybe to your your point, you could spend um a trillion dollars on quantum. There There aren't because um you need somebody You need a human to like wire the refrigerators and the optics tables that you need to operate this stuff. The bottleneck is really around talent. And so, that's where the prioritization of where you're spending the money, to my mind, is everything. Because actually, you could throw money at this all day long to your point, and maybe you need it, but you'd overfeed, and you wouldn't get there anyways. >> It's sort of like AI. Talent is the problem. Hundred million dollar salaries from Meta for every quantum researcher. >> It It is talent, and this is the only thing Like my my mental if I was to create a metric to follow for this, um I think of everything in terms of like iteration loops or cycle times. There's a commercial cycle time, right? Which is how long's it take for you to sell the company for lots of money. That's super important. Um cuz that gets venture investors excited, new startup founders with great ideas excited. But then there's the other cycle time around technology. How long does it take for go from an idea to a widget to a product and test that in a relevant environment? And the reason I I focus on that is actually a lot of bottlenecks there. Some of it's talent, right? That can be a real bottleneck to it. But actually a lot of it can be access to certain technical services and certain capabilities like a superconducting fab, certain capabilities like um certain types of 35 fab, certain capabilities like scaled cryogenics. That it's actually not necessarily bottlenecked by talent. It's bottlenecked by the right sort of policy. So that's That's the like model that I I have for it. Um >> Well, well, this gets back to the discussion of if if we moved from science phase to engineering phase, not discounting the future science that has to happen. But um do we have the right institutions for that scale up? And I think in the semiconductor space, like there is agreement that it's gotten way too hard and expensive to take an idea and translate into a prototype. Um because prototyping is expensive and you need all this exquisite equipment and materials and whatnot. Um the same is true I think basically in the in the quantum space that I idea to prototype is hard because prototype is expensive and needs this unique tool set. So to talk to us about what has happened and what else needs to happen to facilitate that that scale up process. >> Yeah. It It's It's a good It's something I was actually chatting with Constanza about um with her quantum supply chain paper. Um uh the short answer is I think we're we have the cards that we have in terms of the institutions in the US, the Western world behind it. Um [snorts] you have fundamental research and there's um we should be thoughtful about the sorts of things that we incentivize with that. You have the free market um and uh the private companies that are racing at this. I think if I could create one institution, it would be an IMEC. And um if folks aren't familiar with that, it's canonical in the semiconductor industry of having institutions that are public-private nonprofit and they focus on this liminal intermediate phase after fundamental R&D and and it's pre-competitive with the market and they just get good at that middle TRL phase. And it turns out that you need to I think have the institutions that all day long they just build that as a craft. And that's both an expertise, that's a capital structure, that's capital that's um you know, physical capabilities themselves. You need specialized instrumentation that's only good at that phase.
